planting.note: Plant tubers 3-4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in spring; emergence is very late (late May to June in most climates), so mark the location to avoid disturbing the tubers while they are dormant.

directions: Plant tubers in cool, humus-rich, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial to full shade in a sheltered spot; this Chinese species is one of the most beautiful of all the jack-in-the-pulpit relatives, producing a pristine white striped spathe with delicate pink-to-green striping that emerges in early summer from the still-bare soil. The large, divided, umbrella-like leaves unfurl after the flower and remain attractive until late summer. Unlike most arisaemas, the tubers are relatively easy to grow and the plant does well in enriched woodland garden soil with reliable summer moisture. It goes completely dormant by late summer and must be marked to avoid digging the tubers accidentally. Hardy to zone 6 with good drainage and mulching; outstanding in dappled shade beneath maples and oaks.

planting.note: Plant tubers 4-5 inches deep in spring in woodland soil; this species emerges very late in the season and needs a long growing period to build up the large tuber required for flowering. Mark the planting site carefully.

directions: Plant in deep, humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Chinese cobra lily is among the most dramatic of all woodland aroids, producing a tall purple-striped green spathe with a conspicuously long, whip-like appendix that extends 12-18 inches beyond the spathe opening, giving the plant an exotic, otherworldly appearance. The large leaf, divided into many radiating leaflets, creates a spectacular tropical effect. In its native habitat in the Himalayan foothills and mountain forests of central China, it grows in moist mountain ravines and forest understory at 4,000-9,000 feet elevation. In cultivation, the large tubers gradually produce offsets that can be separated after several years. The species performs best in the Pacific Northwest, Upper South, and mild-winter regions of zones 7-10.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seeds 3-4 months in the refrigerator and sow in early spring. Mountain maple grows naturally in cool, moist, rocky woodland soils in deep shade.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; mountain maple is the most shade-tolerant native maple in eastern North America, naturally growing as a small understory tree or large shrub in the deep shade of mixed northern hardwood forests, along rocky mountain streams, and on cool north-facing slopes. The upright flower spikes (distinctive among maples) appear in June after the leaves unfurl. Fall color is outstanding — brilliant scarlet and orange. This is an excellent choice for naturalizing in shaded woodland gardens in cooler climates and is critically important as winter food for moose, who browse the twigs and bark. Not heat-tolerant; best in zones 2-6 where summers stay cool.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in shade; softwood cuttings taken in early summer can be rooted under mist. Seeds require 3-4 months of cold stratification before germinating.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; vine maple is the most shade-tolerant native tree on the Pacific Coast, growing as a multi-stemmed understory tree or large shrub in the dense Douglas-fir and western red cedar forests of the Pacific Northwest from British Columbia to northern California. The circular, 7-9-lobed leaves turn brilliant orange, scarlet, and gold in autumn, making it one of the premier fall-color plants of the region. In deep shade, the stems often lean, twist, and arch to seek light, creating a sculptural form used in Japanese-inspired gardens. The small red flowers appear in spring and the winged samaras ripen in summer. It adapts better to garden conditions than most native Pacific Northwest trees and is excellent for shaded Pacific Coast gardens.
sources: University of British Columbia Botanical Garden

planting.note: Plant small bulbs (bulbils) in fall for spring growth; often self-establishes readily from the aerial bulbils produced at the top of the flower stem. In most of North America, crow garlic has naturalized and may already be present in lawns and disturbed areas.

directions: Plant small bulbs in fall in any well-drained soil in full sun; crow garlic is a European native now widely naturalized across North America that produces hollow, grass-like leaves in early spring with a strong garlic scent, followed by a flowering head that bears both flowers and small aerial bulbils. All parts are edible: the young leaves can be used like chives, the bulbs like mild garlic, and the aerial bulbils can be used as a seasoning. As a foraged plant, it is found in lawns, meadows, and roadsides throughout the eastern US. Medicinally, it shares the antimicrobial and cardiovascular properties of cultivated garlic. Note that the plant can be invasive in lawns and pastures; if controlled harvesting is desired rather than naturalization, grow in containers to contain the spreading bulbils.
